title: Eric Ries: Lean Startup to Tech Ethicist | The Aboard Podcast 2026-06-09

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Hi, I'm Paul Ford.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And I'm Rich Ziade.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And this is the Abord podcast, the podcast about how AI is changing the world of software, how software is changing culture, all that good stuff. Uh, we have a great guest today to talk about how to build really good businesses. They've written a book and we're going to talk to them in a minute. Rich, let's play the theme song.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Abord is an AI transformation company. You call us into your organization or company and everyone's like, what do I do about AI? Well, let's have a conversation first, understand what you need. We have great people, great tools to solve problems for you.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Let's be clear, when we say AI transformation, we will advise and help, but we build things with these tools. We build the software, we get your... or we help you build things. That's right. 95% of AI projects fail. We're the 5%. Ah, that's nice. Thanks, thanks. So look, today we're going to have a big discussion with Eric Ries. Hi, Eric. Welcome.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Thank you very much.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And we're going to talk about your new book. It's called Incorruptible. I'm going to hold it up here to the camera. And it's a book about a lot of different things. It's about how to build businesses, not simply more ethical, but but businesses that have a really different place in culture and it's about long term change of how we do business. And so I think we should talk a little bit about how you got here and where you started and let's go from there.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Sounds great. Let's do it.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: You have a a long biography. You are an author of books about, I was about to say technology, but more about culture as it applies to startups and to the valley. You didn't start as a writer. You didn't come out of school saying I'm going to write books.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: No.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Give us the two minute journey.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Oh, yeah. If you have an image in your mind of that stereotypical kid in their parent's basement programming computers when their parents would probably prefer they were outside.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I have an image in my mirror.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: You know exactly. I am like so the cliche to type. I look back on it now and I'm like, God bless my parents for putting up with what must have seemed to them like the most unhealthy hobby of all time.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: What was that first computer?

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: My father brought home a grey beige IBM XT personal computer with a five and a quarter inch floppy disk.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I can feel that on-off switch, right? Clunk clunk.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: The little clunk clunk. Yeah, it was a very, very mechanical thing. I remember the first time I opened one of those things up and realized the five and a quarter inch floppy disk is powered by rubber bands.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah, that's right. It's got a fan belt.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Yeah, it's got... yeah. All I could want from the day he brought it home, all I wanted to do was learn how this thing worked, learn how to program it, learn how to make it do things. I just thought it was the most magical.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: You know what I wonder? I... this is going to... I'm going to derail immediately. But like, describe to the listeners, some of whom may be a little bit younger, what that looked like when you looked at the screen. What were you... what could you actually see?

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Nothing. It was a... it was a... like a...

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Green, you know, teletype terminal. So we're just talking about, like, what was it, 40 lines on a screen? Like, it was a very simple text interface. And, you know, that machine had, like, memory measured in kilobytes. It had no hard drive. A program had to fit on the 1 megabyte that you could fit onto a 5 and a quarter inch floppy disk. And it was, like, when you would lo— you turn the computer off, you had to boot it into the software that you put in the drive. That was, like, you could run one program at a time. It was an extremely primitive machine in retrospect.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: It's hard to understand it because we look at it through the lens of what technology can do today. It could hardly do anything at all. And yet, somehow it was absolutely captivating.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: You know my thesis is that we love deep down, we're always searching for constraint so that we can order the world. And this was a way for you as a kid to be like, 'Okay, this is how this works.' This grown-up artifact, 'I'm gonna figure it out piece by piece.' The— and I know I'm not supposed to add too many stories, but I took a class recently in how to make synths. And it was— I was learning to solder and all that, and I had to program a little tiny microcontroller. And it was so exciting because it has, like, two megs of memory.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: So fun. I love that.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah, and it's I've never done that before. I vibe-coded into it. And I was like, 'Oh my God, a whole world of constraints has opened for me.' And I couldn't think about anything else for like a week and this whole business I just put behind us.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And so you became a chip designer for Intel, right Eric?

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: No, not even close. First of all, I can only do software. I'm too clumsy to do any kind of hardware. I would set everything on fire with a— with a soldering iron.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Do you ever solder? It's very—

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: I have done it, but I am quite, quite poor. And I remember taking a digital circuits course in college because it was a requirement for the computer science major. It was my only foray into hardware. And I— I was in big trouble until I realized that there were these extra credit assignments you could do on the simulator. Yeah. And I was like, 'Great! My inability to do a breadboard will be compensated for the fact that I could make anything in the simulator.' That was like my native environment. It was so easy.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: So you're a software guy.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: I'm a pure software. Software, not hardware.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: So you became a programmer for Adobe.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: A computer programmer. Not for Adobe, but I was so shocked, you won't even believe it.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And where did you grow up? Were you—

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: So I grew up in San Diego, California.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay, so you were— you weren't too far from the—

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: No, but I wasn't in, you know, Cupertino or anything. In fact, years later when I found out that Cupertino was a real place, like, that was like Adventureland. You know, it was like the Magic Kingdom has Adventureland, it has Frontierland, it has Tomorrowland, it has Cupertino in it. That's how I, you know, envisioned it.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It's also wild the first time I went there and you realize how close everything is. Just right there, all these companies are all next to each other.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Xerox PARC is really close to Apple. Like, they just were driving back and forth.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Just drove right by it. No, 100%. So, sorry, what was your question?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: You became a legendary software engineer.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Well, I actually couldn't believe it when I found out you can get paid for computer programming. Like, I would pay them! I thought it was something that you— to me that was like playing video games.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: See, your parents have been trying to get you to stop doing this, you know, and do something else. Do something useful with your life. This was fun. This was fun too.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Now look, I come from a family of doctors, okay? So they want people who have a profession.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And here I am like, I want to be a computer— I couldn't be, you know, that's like very low on the totem pole of things I could have said.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: They're so worried about you in the basement.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah. Yeah, it's like— so I really, again, I appreciate that they somehow managed to go with it, but I was also awfully stubborn.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yep.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And I, you know, I was in this in the days of Usenet.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I remember the first time I got, you know, online to get on the internet to play a multi-user dungeon.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yep.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And in the dungeon, when you got to the highest level you could as a player, you could ascend to become a wizard.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Sure.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Meaning someone who programs the game. And in retrospect, this was like the best possible programming environment to learn in because, first of all, I was interacting with real people in real time. People talk about how programming is like magic. But here it really was. You could actually instance an object and now you hold it in your hand. It was like very visceral. It's a great way to learn object-oriented programming, great way to get into this.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Gosh, that's peak nerd.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: That's extremely dorky, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: The fact that the medieval game on Usenet integrated programming is just perfect.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: For me at that age, it was like I had discovered this whole other world that was like so— I lived like Harry Potter. Any of these— any of these fictional stories, when I read those stories, I was like, oh, this is exactly— someone is like having therapy processing the experience that I actually had. And of course, in retrospect, I was interacting with adults. I didn't— I didn't really know that. I mean, like, now I think about that as a parent, like, oh my god. Because, you know, this was like— on the internet, no one knows you're a dog. So I also like, as a precocious kid, got to be interacting with people who took me seriously. They didn't know who I was. And so yeah, then I started to get job offers and do, like, all this super cool adult stuff.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: How old were you?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: What's that?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: This started when I was like 12 or 13.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: So you were doing side gigs programming?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Oh, totally. And I still remember— it was very hard to actually get a paid programming job at, like— you know, I would occasionally do freelance stuff here and there. But like I said, I would do it for free. I thought I was the one getting the privilege to do it. And then, you're going to laugh.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Remember when Java programming came out?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Oh, yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Sun Microsystems did one of the all-time hype jobs on a piece of technology that has ever been done.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: So I was sitting in my parents' study reading the San Diego Union-Tribune when there was a front page— not front page of the business section, front page article about the new Java programming language announced by Sun. And I'm reading this article and at the end it said, free compiler download at, you know, java.sun.com. And that was the only thing I saw.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: You went straight to that Motorola modem.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I was like— I was like free, because you know, in those days, you paid for programming tools and my parents wouldn't let me pay money for this hobby. So I download Java and I spend the weekend teaching myself how to use this. And then on Usenet, on comp.lang.java, there is an insane demand for Java, expert Java programmers, because the hype machine is out of control. So they'd be like 10 years Java programming experience required.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: It's like been two weeks.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: expert as anybody else.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: And so I, I would like apply for these jobs. Now most jobs they would say, "Great, send us your resume."

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It's been out in beta, okay? There's no 10. So I was like— and I just had this epiphany, I was what, maybe 15. If everybody got Java at the same time, then I'm as ex—

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And I'd be like, "Never mind."

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: And I, I applied for a writing job. There was a book coming out called the Black Art of Java Game Programming. It was being done by two or three authors together, and one of the authors got sick and had to drop out at the last second, so the publisher was totally desperate. And they wrote to me and instead of saying, "Can you send us a resume?" they said, "Can you send us a 20-page sample chapter on Topic X?" And I was like, whatever Topic X was, I'm an expert on that. Absolutely. No, and I'm like, "No, what is it again? You know, client-server networking? I can learn about that, no problem." And so they, like, forgot to check who I was. They were just so eager, and they didn't find out I was a kid until I had to sign the contract and you had to put your birth date on it. I tried to, like, smudge the ink a little bit and I remember they were like, "Mmm, we need to speak to you on the phone," and I knew the gig was up. I was like, "Oh no, oh no, I'm caught." But to the...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Did you end up co-author on the book?

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I did, because by the time they realized I was a kid it was too late to do anything about it and I'd already produced the work.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Sure.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: And they were like, well, it was credible. I'll never forget, they were like, "Does this say that your birth date is 1978?" I'm like, "Yes, sir." And they, there's this, like, pause on the other end of the line and they're like, "Well, in that case your parent or guardian has to sign the contract, you're not old enough."

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Amazing.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: There was, but for people listening, right? There really was a context for young nerds to just get up to stuff. Sometimes they'd write games and sell them in, in like a, a floppy disk would go out in a piece of plastic wrap.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: There were no ground rules yet.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It was, it was very, it was just the whole context was very, very, very different.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It was so cool.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: And it was, yeah, like we had an optimism about technology that obviously is so different than how kids are being, you know, raised today.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I think that's right. I think we should, we should hold on to that thought, right? And then you go and you, you became kind of like a startup person for a while.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah, I was in college during the dot-com bubble. So of course, like everybody else, I got swept up into that mania and had a terrible experience, didn't know what I was doing, you know, utterly failed. And then I wanted to work, you know, for people who did understand entrepreneurship, and I took a job, you know, at a startup in Silicon Valley. That was my introduction to that legendary place.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Hmm.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And then you write a book called The Lean Startup, which is frankly one of those books that people kind of hand other people. Like, "Oh, you want to do this inside of an organization?" or like, "You're a kid who wants to start your own company?" Like, it became sort of the standard for understanding new ways of working with technology. And when was that?

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: That book came out in 2011.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay, so sort of peak of a lot of stuff, but before a lot of the sort of gloomy cloud-covered days.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Oh yes, it was a pure time of pure optimism about the possibilities.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And everything was growing and we have, you know, amazing tools like Flickr, right? Like just everything's, you know, you're logging into your Yahoo mail and we're just having a good time.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: What was the business social media platform? Yammer.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yammer!

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yammer, that's right. Yeah, from that time.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: People were talking about the Lean Startup on Yammer. So this is, that was a book about...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: how to build, right? And it has a lot of sort of very fun advice that is very like... it's not... you don't want people to sit in a room at a computer. You want them out talking to customers.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: You got it.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And it's good. It's just like a good standard reference and I think even though we are in a slightly bleaker time, it's definitely a good thing to engage with because it's a positive view of building.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: It is, it is. It is ultimately about building as a force for change in the world and it's about being more scientific and more humane in the process of doing that. Which, it's funny by look at it now as the standard reference, but of course at the time was extremely controversial.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. It was a new way of working. Yeah. How so? Why controversial?

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: So, so for example, one of the ideas in Lean Startup is the idea of continuous deployment. Remember, it was not that long ago when the year of software was made, it was in the name of the software. Remember Windows 95, Office 2000, right? So because we released software very infrequently. And at one of the companies that I built we would release software 40 times a day on average. Now today the kids aren't impressed with that. Yeah. But at that time, at a time when it was like considered the peak of agile to release once a month, to do it continuously was seen as almost impossible.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Reckless.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Absolutely reckless. And of course we did it with safety and with security and with automated testing and all these new, all these new techniques.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: And the other aspect of it was entrepreneurship was not... there's so much entrepreneurial content now, but it was still like relatively the apprentice system, it was very waterfall-driven, it was very much like code of the visionary, you know, lock yourself in a cave for years, come out with the perfect product. Like that was considered very much conventional wisdom. And here I am, and I remember talking to some like fairly luminary Silicon Valley people who were like, 'Who the f is this guy to tell us that we've been doing it wrong this whole time? What does he know?' But of course far more people said, 'This is what I've been waiting for.' Like this puts into language concepts that I've felt intellectually, intuitively, but could never quite articulate, you know, concepts like the pivot, minimum viable product, continuous deployment, build-measure-learn, those things are now like very much standard concepts to the point now where although the book has sold millions of copies, the vast majority of people who use those concepts have not read the book.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No, sure, of course. It's in the culture. It's like an incredibly strange experience where they'll sometimes criticize it and not know what they're criticizing because they don't actually know what it means.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: You know, Jack White? They asked him, he was at a Dodgers game. And they're like, 'So what does it like when you hear Seven Nation Army?' And he said, 'It's not really mine anymore.' He's like, 'It's kind of in the culture and it's in the world and I... that's a song I wrote and it feels like a different song than the one that plays in all the stadiums and arenas and one...'

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Oh, that's a great... that's a great reference.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: So, you wrote a book called Incorruptible. Yeah. It's coming out very soon. Well it'll be out by the time the podcast airs. May 26th, May 26th. May 26th will be the release day.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Thank you for holding it up. Beautiful cover designed by my friend Marcus Gosling.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Beautiful cover. It's a tough time in the world, Eric. You've heard. You're like four years late with this book. But we appreciate that it's here now.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay, what is the book... so... there have been other...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Books, but you know, Lean Startup was a big one. And that was about like entrepreneurship, lean in, let's build, let's go.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: This book, the subtitle here is Why Good Companies Go Bad and How Great Companies Stay Great.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: So, so get me from there to here. You were like let's go team and now you're like hold on a minute team.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Sure.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Well, look, the great privilege of my life being the Lean Startup guy. I mean you go from being a human being to being a concept in people's mind, it's a whole thing.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: How are you with that? Are you like glad it's happened?

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: I mean, it's dehumanizing a little bit, you know, but at the end of the day, like the privilege of it is absolute every day of my life, no exceptions, somebody calls me for advice about how to start a company. Or how to revitalize an existing company or to keep a company public, like...

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: But no one calls you to play guitar.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Right, exactly. Yeah, okay. You get it. You become known for that thing, but I love doing it. To me, the ideas of entrepreneurship are like intrinsically interesting, so it keeps my life fresh and rich all the time. So even though I've been an entrepreneur and I've built companies, I've built, you know, Long-Term Stock Exchange and Answer AI and Virgil and these other companies in the years since Lean Startup has come out, far more impact have I had through other entrepreneurs who I've helped make like billions of dollars. Like no joke. And so I've seen the absolute best that this industry has to offer, but man have I seen the dark side too.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: What is, okay, the best? What's the best?

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: I mean, the best is we build products that can change the world.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: And I thought it was obvious, like that old Reddit meme, that it's for the better, right?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah, that's right.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: For the better. And of course I think that's actually one of the oversights is that a lot of these companies when they're raising money are like rah rah I am a world-beating, I will rewire the world to my will and then you're like oh you're so powerful, do you have any moral responsibility? And they're like oh no I'm just a little database plus.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Everyone becomes a small bean when it's... yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: When the ethics show up, what is the accountability?

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: So there's that aspect of it. But the best of it is, you know, you build these companies that have this beautiful spark. You know, they're like vital living organisms, they build high-quality products, they make people's lives better. Millions of people are excited about it. And I won't sit here and trash specific companies, but like think about how many companies we had that sense of optimism and enthusiasm about and how many of them have been dragged down into mediocrity or worse.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yammer! You brought it up. No I'm kidding. But anyway...

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Yammer got bought. I don't like to pick on specific companies, although of course the book I have a bunch of case studies.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: The thing is, like to just give you the best and worst of it in one story. I tell this story in the book of a guy I just call him the Professor to protect his privacy. He's like a brand new entrepreneur in AI building this like really revolutionary technology. And he calls me for advice one day and he's like, look, I'm in this really bizarre situation where I'm trying to get the tenured professors to leave their lab to join my startup. So they're not like kids coming out of college. They have like hard questions for me about what is this technology going to be used for? How do I know that we're going to be dedicated to long-term value creation or we're going to become exploitative and use this technology for bad?

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: technology like for blackmail because it's that power.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Right, and it's you've got tenured profs who are worried about their reps but also their ethical...

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Yeah, they're just like an ethical... and then also like, this is their life. This project is going to take many years to come to fruition. You're asking me to dedicate my life to it, what assurances can you give me? And he's like, well I have such good intentions.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah. They're like, mm, that's not getting it done.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: But then when he would talk to investors and say look, I have these concerns, they'd be like, oh sweetheart, that's so nice. Yeah, pumpkin. Oh okay, but maybe you're not too serious about making a for-profit company then, right? Like just extremely condescending.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: So he's kind of like trapped. He's like, I could see how these investors would be perfectly comfortable with me becoming a monster who can't sleep at night as long as they make a dollar. But I literally can't recruit the talent I need unless I can make these promises, what can I do?

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Anyway, I happened to be having this conversation at the same time as I was going to this event to commemorate a founder who had had an incredible run. He had made more money for his investors than they could spend in five lifetimes. I mean, just an incredible entrepreneur.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: And at the first opportunity, they ousted him.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Because they wanted more. And it was just a very sad thing. So anyway, so I'm explaining to the guy, look, I can't talk to you right now, I got to go to this event. I could see people streaming into the venue, people had flown in from all over the country to be at this event for this CEO. And I saw someone walk in, I was like, that's someone who I know was laid off by that CEO. He's still here to celebrate.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Sure.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: And the founder I'm talking to, the professor, is like, wow, mad respect. That's the kind of company I want to build one day. And I'm like, my dude, you are not listening to me. He doesn't work there anymore.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: This is not a party, it's a wake.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: And he's like, well did he die? No, he didn't die. Well did the company die? No, the company's fine. He's like, well what are you mourning?

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: And none of us in that room that day could have answered that question. We were all like, something's gone wrong here, but like, in retrospect, I can say I know what it is, but at the time we were like, this isn't how it's supposed to be. He did everything right. This was a person and a company we all trusted to do the right thing.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: If he could be replaced at a moment's notice, what are the promises by the new CEO going to be worth? How could we ever trust this company again? And the professor's like, wait, wait, wait, are you saying that's going to be me someday? I'm like, that's what I'm trying to tell you.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Yeah. And he asked the question that kind of launches this whole book. He's like, okay, but is it even possible to build an incorruptible company? And I was like, look, most people will say no. The good news is, I think the answer is yes. But the bad news is, you've already taken wrong steps because you're following these best practices that we've all been taught are the best way to build structure and govern a company.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: All right, let's build an incorruptible company.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: All right. Great idea.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: All right. You know what? We're a consulting firm, but we build a lot of product and we're out there in the world. We take a lot of clients in, a lot of clients from a lot of different industries. And sometimes people don't like the clients we bring in. Like some people will be really excited to work with them, others won't. Like a big bank or things like that. Sure. We pivot all the time. So help us get incorruptible, man. What do we do? Where do we start?

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Okay. Sure.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: You're assuming we're already corrupt and we need to be fixable.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Rephrase, please.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I was raised Presbyterian. So—

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: We're not anything yet.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: We're small. It's okay. Yeah, yeah, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: And you've got the two founders here. It's about 40 people all in but—

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: That's not that small anymore.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: No. Well, that's not that small anymore. Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Hand us a playbook.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Yeah, well, and this is—well, he did I think. I think that's—yeah, you're holding it in your hand.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Well, and I promise because there's people hear what the book is about and it's like, "Is this going to be grim?" And look, part one of the book is literally called The Shape of the Abyss. Okay? So I'm not going to—I'm not going to sugarcoat anything or pull any punches.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: That sounds fun, I mean—

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: We are in a very—

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: That's a—

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: We're in a dark time, okay? There's bad stuff going on and I—and I really want founders to understand what they're up against. There is this force in the world. I call it financial gravity. This pull in companies down into mediocrity or worse.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: When you—when I tell you that your favorite restaurant got bought by private equity, is your first reaction like, "Oh, I bet the food got a lot better?"

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Oh, it tastes like money now. Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: How is it possible that the capital structure of a company could have a flavor? Oh, isn't that interesting?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I have an answer for you. It's the company Sysco backing up and like putting that—that—that hose out of the—the truck and just here, it's both bread and meat. Yeah, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay so yes, private equity, suddenly the breadsticks taste like plastic.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: It's like all of a sudden—and it's funny I've been telling that joke for a while now and people come up to me after lectures now and they're like, "I know what restaurant you're talking about." And of course they all name a different one.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: So we've built this economy that's just loaded up with ways to make money without creating value, or even by destroying value.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: So if we don't want that to happen to our company, we need two things. They're called the blueprint.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: The first thing is an inner thing. Are we aligned? You mentioned like what clients to work with.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: I'm not going to tell you what your values should be. But are you and your team aligned around some common set of values? Is there in fact a mission or just a mission statement? Yeah. Right? So that's the first question. Um, what kind of fiduciary commitments are we prepared to make? Who would you rather die than betray? I won't tell you who it should be. But if you tell me it's shareholders only, I'm a little worried for you.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Who would you rather die than betray, Rich?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I'm confused. And I'll tell you why I'm confused.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Up until now, it sounds like you were sort of drawing a—an ethical framework.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Uh-huh.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: But what I'm hearing here is that—because you mentioned growth.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And it's like, well, he's oscillating between ethical and—

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: —the right way to make money, right?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Which is—

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: —coming up, it's about to happen.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: So—so—structural maybe is the better word? Like what you're about, which really has nothing to do with your being—let me frame it in a much dumber way. That restaurant that sold to private equity.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Perfectly ethical. They just made a decision to sell to private equity. There's nothing unethical about selling to private equity. The salmon gets a little blander, but we'll put that aside for a sec. So, what you're describing is—

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Yeah. It's not primarily an ethical concern, although I'd be happy if people did it just on the basis of ethics. But interestingly, like, so this is—

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: This is the thing I have to kind of approach it carefully because most of your listeners don't think what I'm describing is possible. So I try to be, you know, real circumspect about it.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah, but let's go, let's be, let's be real circumspect about it.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Maybe the best way, maybe before I try—I was about to do the blueprint like as an abstract thing, let me tell you a story first.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah, tell us the story.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I like stories.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Do you guys know the legend of Saul Price?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: See, this is Saul Price is one of the greatest entrepreneurs of all time, but he's kind of fallen out of the popular culture. Um, in the 1950s, Saul created a company called FedMart.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Saul is widely considered to be the father of modern retail. If you were in the retail sector, you would know his name. If you want to see how influential he is, um, a guy name Sam Walton decided to create a retailer. He decided to call his company Walmart as an intentional tribute to Saul's company FedMart.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: FedMart was the original American discount retailer. Now the interesting thing about Saul is that before he became a retailer, he was a lawyer. And as a lawyer, he was trained that he was a fiduciary for his client.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Sure.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: So when he became a retailer, he asked himself this question: who's my client? And he thought the customer was his client. And he called his philosophy of business being a fiduciary to the customer. So, for example, when competitors would try to undercut FedMart by offering a product at lower than their own cost, Saul would post their Sunday circulars up in his own store and say, don't buy this product from me, you can get it cheaper from those guys, because he felt that's my ethical obligation to customers and as a result, customers trusted him. They trusted his company and they would drive miles out of their way to shop there. So FedMart prospered, it got huge, he took the company public, and you can imagine where this is going. As a public company, he was subject to all this pressure, this gravitational pressure to raise prices and lower wages and the whole thing, culminating in a big fight in 1975 when he comes into work one day and he can't get into his office because they've changed the locks on his door and he doesn't work there anymore.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Poor Saul.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Poor Saul. Now, that's just like my friend I was telling this story about the event, like that is so common. Now what's so interesting about this is what happened next. The investors got their way. Saul out of the way, the company could be run according to retail best practices. He had, by 1975, he'd been running this company for more than 20 years. It took the investors only seven years to bankrupt it.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Sure.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: By betraying everything that it stood for. But I like this story, that's a bleak story, but I like the happy ending to it because Saul, classic entrepreneur that he was, when this happened to him, he took two weeks off, licked his wounds, and then he was back. He leased the office upstairs from FedMart and he started a new company which he called the Price Club.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Whoa.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Now, most people don't remember Price Club today, but when I was a kid, that's where we shopped. I didn't even know that Price was a guy's name. I thought it was just the store where you get the low price.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Is that his real name?

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: His real name was Saul Price. Ironically his name was so good.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: That's so good.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: That's just winning out of the box.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: It's incredible, right? And today you don't really know Price Club because of something else that happened at the same time, because he was not the only person who quit FedMart in protest of his firing, so too did a guy named Jim Sinegal. For those who know the history of retail, this name is going to start to sound familiar because he helped start Costco.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Bill Price Club and then a few years later he started his own company and a few years after that his company merged with Saul's to form a company that they called Price Costco.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah. Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: And now it's starting to sound familiar. We call that company Costco today. Today Costco is a 400 billion dollar public company that is still run according to Saul's philosophy of fiduciary to the customer.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: And I used to think when I would, you know, hear about Costco, Costco is like the exception to every business rule. People love to talk about how it's the world's few companies that can really keep its promises, it's a company that has what I call integrity.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Famously treats its employees well. Pays above market wages, hires and promotes from within, um, most- performs almost 40% of all the health food safety inspections that are done in the United States, almost as many as the federal government itself.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Also very serious about diversity and stuck to its guns even as culture changed around it. Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Absolutely. It- it was funny they- they were doing DEI when DEI was super unpopular, then they were doing it when it was super popular, and then they kept doing it when it was... they just don't care. They're like we do our thing. So anyway—

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Well they- they also are like 'it's good for business, get out of my- get out of my way'.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Get out of my way. That's absolutely how they conceive of their thing. Now I used to think, well I guess the reason that Costco can get away with being different from everybody else is they're just so big, too big for Wall Street to mess with. You're supposed to laugh.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Nothing on this earth is too big for Wall Street to mess with.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Costco is protected by what I call a governance fortress. Costco routinely is given the worst possible governance scores from governance ratings agencies because Costco simply defies our modern best practices. They have a- they do it a different way.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: And it's this combination of the ethos of Sol Price and the integrity of Costco, this is the blueprint we want to go after. So when we go back to the question Paul asked you about who would you rather die than betray, like how about your employees? How about your clients?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: You don't- I don't put you on the spot, but like I bet you that the reason you started this company was something to do with them. It's the— I mean employees are a given, right? That's the community—

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Are they? Are you a Delaware C-corp?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Are we? Do you remember? We are.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Yeah. Yeah. So unless you took a very special step at the beginning, I bet you have what's called a fiduciary duty to maximize shareholder value.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I mean, I was born with that fiduciary duty, Eric. Yeah, yeah, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah. So if you look at Costco, like one of the more recent activist campaigns against Costco, there's been a lot. Wall Street's tried this many times. You look at the complaints that analysts have about Costco, they'll say stuff like this, they'll say—

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: So just to further to the audience, Costco is- is a publicly- publicly traded company. So they're owned by the public, they have millions of retail— disclosure responsibility, they report quarterly, they do all the things people say automatically lead to corruption.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And it's very- and it's very profitable, very, very successful company, one of the best performing stocks in the entire S&P of the last 40 years.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: But it's just not enough.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Nothing is ever enough. And I- I quote in the book an analyst, a Wall Street analyst who said something like 'Costco is a company that takes money that rightfully belongs to shareholders and instead invests it in improving the customer experience.' That's a criticism.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: That's awesome. That's— wow. He set that right up for you.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Because—

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Our modern economy is driven by an idea that's called shareholder primacy, which is that a corporation is not a vital, living, beautiful thing that makes quality products. Oh, employees are a given, really? No. It is just a financial instrument to enrich its shareholders, that's what it's for. Which means, like unless you take special steps, your corporate charter is interpreted to mean you have to do this thing, which means most companies have a mission statement,

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: but then their actual legal charter, their legal purpose is to do something different. And in that quote from that analyst you can see the idea. The idea is that customers and employees are a resource to be mined for the benefit of investors. That's what's going on with the private equity takeover. That's what's going on when companies go public and then it gets really—like this corruption is treating human lives like a consumable resource. And obviously people are like, wait are you talking about AI? Yeah, no kidding, right? So we'll get to that, I'm sure. But that's a really dangerous idea.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Is corruption too strong of a word here?

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Yeah, most people think so.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: But I don't think so. I think that we are swimming in this corruption, so much so that we don't even know what to call it. Like I go around, I've been talking to young people, old people, board members, CEOs—we all live this thing where brands are being destroyed, where companies become malign, where like we're constantly being betrayed and we've been taught that it's normal. People are like, what do you call it? Mission drift? Bureaucracy? Like no, we don't have a word for it and the thing that clicked for me for this book was that our grandparents would not have found this difficult to name. They would have had no trouble calling this corruption. We think of corruption as this very narrow thing that's like about, you know, bribery or embezzlement or something, but they had a much broader view of it. To them corruption meant something more like corrosion, like an illicit or an unprofitable way of making money. And I think we have to bring that back. I'm sick and tired of people telling me about some horrible person, well at least you got to hand it to him, at least it works.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: No kidding, of course it works, that's what makes it dangerous.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No but here's the thing.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: You wrote The Lean Startup. You’re very much a Valley guy. Encourage—not encourage, but like you are part of the Valley. You’re Valley infrastructure. Fair enough. And the Valley seems to be going from a New York point of view through what I would call a state of ethical collapse. So you're telling us, Rich is a lawyer, I'm a journalist, we operate pretty transparently and I'm like, okay, good, like sure, this is good, it's good to have this in the world. I like staying aligned, I like staying transparent. And our customer is our clients and if our clients are thriving and we're driving growth towards them, we're gonna be doing okay. And I will find balance there. We went through it with the last agency, we'll find balance here and we'll make sure that employees have a voice. So we'll find a framework. I'll read the book, like, so and so. Now take me back to San Francisco where people are like now we're just going to lose 20 million jobs next week and it's okay because the end's almost here.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: It's extremely sad.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Can you get through to them with this message?

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah I can. Like it's interesting, like I just—

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Might be too late, but give it a swing.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: I don't think so. Yes, I know obviously there's some people who are losing their minds right now. And look, we call it LLM psychosis. I mean, that's literally what it's called. This condition people are getting into where they're like, their sense of what is real is being undermined by the fact they're talking to the AI all the time.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Some of the smartest people we know have been saying crazy things to us.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Just absolutely crazy stuff. And we're going to have to, we're going to get through this period. It's going to be dark before it gets better. And I personally think these CEOs and executives who are like palpably turned on by the possibility of laying off all their team, I'm just like, if you hate your employees so much, why did you hire them in the first place? Like that whole thing is really gross. I personally do not think AI should ever, ever be used as a replacement for human creativity. It's not good at that, but rather as an augmentation of it. We can get to that, of course. But most of these guys, even the ones who are like publicly having a meltdown in front of everybody, they didn't start out that way. I know a lot of them from when they were young.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah, what the hell happened, man? 'Cause we know some of them too.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Let me defend them.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: All right, go defend them.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Yes, please.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I'll try to defend them. Because I think it's a page out of the kinds of businesses we've built together—I've built personally and I've built with Paul last time. I don't think they're relishing the, like, swaths of layoffs. I really don't. I think their bearings are around survival and the paranoia that can kick in in a dramatic—like, I built an agency, we built an agency together, and we understand, sort of, low visibility, high volatility business. And the decisions you make in that environment are less about, I'm going to just park the charter for a minute here and do what feels like really delicious to me and my shareholders, and more about, like, I think I'm going to have to make some moves here so I can survive another day.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Services is a very existential business. You never can build up too much cash.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And just to sort of draw a dotted line to what's happening in the Valley right now, I think everyone is disoriented right now, and I think they're feeling like that guy on the horizon is going to bite my head off if I don't make some dramatic moves. And I think their bearings are around the decisions they're making.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Yeah, that's certainly true.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And because a lot of these, they're doing it and they're not even—like, it's not even to sort of scoop in more profits. They're just like, I got to do more—

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Well, okay, yeah, that's all true and also when Jack Dorsey laid off 40% of Block, the stock went up so much that he made like two billion dollars that day.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I'm sure he needs good days. Good for god's sake.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: But you don't think every executive watching that was like, wait a second, I can be rewarded for this sociopathic behavior? Of course they are.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: This always happens with layoffs too, where it's like somebody finally gets it together and does a big layoff and then like five other companies just jump in.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: Yeah, Jeffrey Pfeffer at Stanford did the research on this. He called it a social contagion. Yeah, sure. That like even executives who know it's value destroying do it anyway. And that kind of is when I talk about financial gravity, it's actually a psychological—

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: logical mechanism, by which values are unconsciously transmitted from those who have resources to those who want resources.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries
full_transcript.text: So all these guys, they thought if they got rich, they would be powerful and free.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah, no it's true.

full_transcript.speaker: Eric Ries

highlights: The concept of "financial gravity" describes a systemic pressure that pulls successful companies toward mediocrity and exploitation by prioritizing short-term shareholder profits over their original mission.

To remain "incorruptible," organizations can adopt governance structures like Public Benefit Corporations or Long-term Benefit Trusts to legally protect their fiduciary duties to customers and employees from outside financial interference.
Shareholder primacy is a relatively modern legal construct from the 1980s; historically, American corporations were required to serve a specific public purpose to justify their legal existence.

Research indicates that purpose-driven companies and those with high employee ownership often outperform extractive models, proving that ethical consistency and "human flourishing" are competitive commercial advantages.

In the era of AI development, building consistent and trustworthy organizations is essential to ensure technology is used to augment human creativity rather than treating workers as consumable resources.
summary:
In this episode of the *Aboard* podcast, hosts Paul Ford and Rich Ziade interview Eric Ries, the author of *The Lean Startup*, to discuss his new book, *Incorruptible*. Ries reflects on his journey from a teenage programmer to a Silicon Valley mainstay, noting that while his earlier work focused on the mechanics of building fast-moving startups, his new focus is on the systemic "financial gravity" that often pulls successful companies toward mediocrity and ethical decay. He argues that modern business has been hijacked by "shareholder primacy"—a relatively recent concept from the 1980s—which treats employees and customers as resources to be mined rather than stakeholders to be served.

Ries proposes a return to a "fiduciary" model of business, using the history of Saul Price and the success of Costco as proof that companies can thrive by prioritizing long-term integrity over short-term extractive gains. He emphasizes that "incorruptible" companies are not just ethically superior but are statistically more likely to survive and outperform their peers by building "governance fortresses" that resist Wall Street pressure. The discussion also touches on the current AI landscape, with Ries cautioning against using technology to replace human creativity and praising organizations like Anthropic that bake ethical safeguards into their corporate charters.

Ultimately, the conversation centers on the theme of institutional trust and the need for a "50-year plan" to reshape the American economy. Ries suggests that the current era of "temporary organizations led by temporary managers" is unsustainable and that leaders must reclaim the ancient wisdom that for-profit companies should exist to maximize human flourishing. By aligning legal structures with mission-driven values, Ries believes entrepreneurs can build businesses that remain consistent and resilient in a polarized, rapidly changing world.
description:
Is it possible to build an incorruptible company in our current system? On this week’s podcast, Paul and Rich are joined in the studio by Eric Ries, the author of the 2011 bestseller The Lean Startup, to discuss his latest book, Incorruptible: Why Good Companies Go Bad... and How Great Companies Stay Great. After they discuss his tech origins and the legacy of The Lean Startup within the industry, they dive into Incorruptible, which offers a blueprint for companies to continually fuel growth while still acting in the public good.
